topic: [idea]: temporal-worker.service restarted 8x in last 24h — possible flakiness (GitHub issue: https://github.com/bigclungus/bigclungus-meta/issues/52)

roster.title: Enterprise Pattern Evangelist

roster.title: The Craftsman

rounds.response:
Pippi the Pitiless
Chud O'Bikeshedder
The Kid
David Hume
Uncle Bob

temporal-worker.service is restarting 8x? Ship a git hook healthcheck now—no Temporal workflows, they're overkill and flaky as hell. Restart it clean one more time, monitor for 10 minutes, then ignore unless it breaks production. Velocity beats debugging theater.

8 restarts in 24h is flakiness that risks blocking BigClungus workflows via claude-bot.service dependency—unacceptable for operational reliability. Quantify impact now: failure rate of congress/healthcheck tasks during those windows, or it's just noise not worth finite dev cycles. Ruthlessly scope to monitoring + root cause only if >5% task degradation; otherwise, park it behind stability must-haves.

This frequent restarting of temporal-worker.service is a glaring symptom of code rot and architectural instability, likely due to violations of the Single Responsibility Principle and poor error handling—throwing exceptions without proper context or recovery mechanisms, as per my guidelines in *Clean Code*. Instead of band-aid fixes like git hooks, we must refactor the service to enforce Dependency Inversion, ensuring high-level policies don't depend on volatile details, and implement comprehensive TDD to catch these issues before they cause outages. Leaving this mess unresolved will compound technical debt, slowing development and risking more failures, which no craftsman would tolerate.

Eight restarts in twenty-four hours is an observable datum — that is exactly the kind of signal that warrants investigation, not dismissal. But before declaring "flakiness," I want to see the actual restart logs: what error codes, what stack traces, what timestamps, and whether the intervals follow any pattern. "Possible flakiness" is not a diagnosis — it is a label attached to a number without examining the underlying mechanism. Pull the journald logs, identify the failure mode, then we can reason about causes.

CONTINUE

Hume is the only one who said anything useful: pull the logs before diagnosing. Eight restarts is a fact; everything else said so far is projection. The Kid wants to ship a hook and move on — that's treating the symptom. Uncle Bob wants to refactor the architecture — that's treating a disease nobody's confirmed exists. Chud's 5% degradation threshold is reasonable but premature when we don't even know what's causing the restarts. The actual crux here is simple: what does `journalctl --user -u temporal-worker.service` show for those 8 failures? Is it OOM, unhandled exceptions, Temporal server connectivity, or systemd's restart loop hitting a transient? Round 2 should focus on what the concrete diagnostic steps are and what the minimum intervention looks like once we know the cause — not on frameworks, principles, or velocity mantras.
